subject: Chemistry
unit: Unit 1: ATOMIC STRUCTURE AND PERIODIC PROPERTIES OF THE ELEMENTS

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.unit: Unit 1: ATOMIC STRUCTURE AND PERIODIC PROPERTIES OF THE ELEMENTS
questions.section: 1.8 Electronic Configurations and the Periodic Table of the Elements
questions.question: Why does atomic radius generally decrease from left to right across a period in the periodic table?

options.A: Increasing effective nuclear charge pulls electrons closer
options.B: Electrons are added to higher energy levels

questions.hint: More protons attract the electron cloud more strongly.
questions.explanation: Across a period, protons increase, increasing effective nuclear charge (Z_eff). Electrons are added to the same shell, so stronger attraction reduces radius. Option D is false (shielding is roughly constant).

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 1: ATOMIC STRUCTURE AND PERIODIC PROPERTIES OF THE ELEMENTS
source_trace.section_anchor: 1.8 Electronic Configurations and the Periodic Table of the Elements
source_trace.content_anchor: Atomic radius generally increases in a group from top to bottom but it decreases in a period from left to right

explanations_tiered.definition: Atomic radius decreases across a period because increasing nuclear charge pulls valence electrons closer to the nucleus.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Effective nuclear charge (Z_eff) increases while shielding remains similar, so electrons experience stronger attraction, shrinking the atom.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Think of a magnet: more protons (positive pull) in the nucleus squeeze the electron cloud tighter.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: From Na to Cl, protons increase from 11 to 17, but electrons add to the same n=3 shell. Z_eff rises, so atomic radius decreases.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Z_eff = Z - S, where S is shielding constant. Across a period, S increases slowly, but Z increases by 1 each step, so Z_eff increases, reducing orbital size.
reasoning_steps: Recall trend: atomic radius decreases left to right.
Consider nuclear charge increase and constant shielding.
Stronger pull → smaller radius.
misconceptions: Some think adding electrons always increases size — but electrons go to same shell, so increased nuclear charge dominates.
Shielding does not change much across a period.

glossary_entry.definition: Atomic radius decreases left to right across a period due to increasing effective nuclear charge.

flashcard.front: Why does atomic radius decrease across a period?
flashcard.back: Increasing effective nuclear charge pulls electrons inward.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Trend of atomic radius across a period? Why?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Decreases; increasing Z_eff pulls electrons closer.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Distance from nucleus to valence electron shell.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Explain general trends in atomic radius within a period
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Increased Z_eff reduces atomic radius.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.unit: Unit 1: ATOMIC STRUCTURE AND PERIODIC PROPERTIES OF THE ELEMENTS
questions.section: 1.8 Electronic Configurations and the Periodic Table of the Elements
questions.question: Which of the following correctly orders the elements by increasing atomic radius?
questions.hint: Atomic radius decreases left to right across a period.
questions.explanation: Across period 2: atomic radius decreases from left to right (largest to smallest): Li > Be > B > C > N > O > F > Ne. So Ne (smallest) < F < O (largest among these three).

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 1: ATOMIC STRUCTURE AND PERIODIC PROPERTIES OF THE ELEMENTS
source_trace.section_anchor: 1.8 Electronic Configurations and the Periodic Table of the Elements
source_trace.content_anchor: atomic radius generally decreases in a period from left to right

explanations_tiered.definition: Atomic radius decreases from left to right across a period due to increasing effective nuclear charge.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Order of increasing radius means smallest first. Ne is smallest, F larger, O largest among these three.
explanations_tiered.beginner: On a row, atoms shrink as you move right. Neon is smallest, oxygen is biggest.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Period 2 radii (pm): O ≈ 73, F ≈ 71, Ne ≈ 70. So Ne < F < O.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Even though Ne has more electrons, its higher Z_eff (due to poor shielding of p electrons) makes it smaller than F and O.
worked_solution: Step 1: Recall trend: atomic radius decreases left to right.
Step 2: Order of elements left to right: O (8), F (9), Ne (10).
Step 3: Leftmost is largest, rightmost is smallest.
Step 4: Increasing radius: Ne (smallest) < F < O (largest).
reasoning_steps: Identify positions: O, F, Ne in period 2.
Left to right: O → F → Ne.
Radius decreases in that order.
Increasing radius: Ne, then F, then O.
misconceptions: Some think Ne should be largest because it has more electrons — but higher Z_eff overcomes electron count.
Noble gases have slightly larger radii than halogens due to full shells, but trend still decreasing.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Across period: radius decreases. Ne < F < O.

glossary_entry.definition: For same period, atomic radius decreases left to right.
glossary_entry.example_equation: Ne < F < O < N < C < B < Be < Li

flashcard.front: Order O, F, Ne by increasing atomic radius.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Rank O, F, Ne by increasing atomic radius.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Measure of atom size; decreases across period.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Remind that Z_eff increases across period.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Rank elements by atomic size within a period
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Ne has smallest radius, then F, then O.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.unit: Unit 1: ATOMIC STRUCTURE AND PERIODIC PROPERTIES OF THE ELEMENTS
questions.section: 1.8 Electronic Configurations and the Periodic Table of the Elements
questions.question: Which element has the highest first ionization energy in Period 3?
questions.hint: Ionization energy increases across a period, with noble gases having the highest.
questions.explanation: Ionization energy increases from left to right across a period. Argon (noble gas) has the highest IE₁ due to its stable full valence shell. Na has the lowest.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 1: ATOMIC STRUCTURE AND PERIODIC PROPERTIES OF THE ELEMENTS
source_trace.section_anchor: 1.8 Electronic Configurations and the Periodic Table of the Elements
source_trace.content_anchor: ionization energies increase with atomic number ... largest values occur for the noble-gas elements

explanations_tiered.definition: First ionization energy is the energy required to remove the most loosely held electron from a gaseous atom.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: IE increases across a period due to increasing Z_eff and decreasing radius. Noble gases have the highest IE because they have a full valence shell.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Argon's electrons are held very tightly — it's hard to steal one, so it has the highest ionization energy.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Period 3 IE order: Na (496) < Mg (738) < Al (578) < Si (787) < P (1012) < S (1000) < Cl (1251) < Ar (1520 kJ/mol). Ar is highest.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Ar has configuration [Ne]3s²3p⁶, a stable octet. Removing an electron disrupts this stability, requiring much more energy than any other Period 3 element.
reasoning_steps: Recall IE increases left to right across a period.
Argon is farthest right in Period 3 (noble gas).
Noble gas has highest IE.
misconceptions: Some think metals have high IE — false; nonmetals and noble gases are higher.
Exceptions exist (e.g., Al lower than Mg), but Ar still highest in period.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: IE highest in period = noble gas (Ar).

glossary_entry.definition: First ionization energy increases across a period, highest for noble gases.

flashcard.front: Which Period 3 element has highest first ionization energy?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Highest first ionization energy in Period 3?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Energy to remove an electron from gaseous atom.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Remind that noble gases have full octets.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Compare ionization energies across a period
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. Argon has highest IE in Period 3 due to stable octet.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.unit: Unit 1: ATOMIC STRUCTURE AND PERIODIC PROPERTIES OF THE ELEMENTS
questions.section: 1.8 Electronic Configurations and the Periodic Table of the Elements
questions.question: Why is the first ionization energy of boron (B) lower than that of beryllium (Be)?

options.A: Boron has a higher effective nuclear charge
options.B: Boron's outermost electron is in a higher energy 2p orbital, which is easier to remove
options.C: Beryllium has a lower nuclear charge
options.D: Boron has a smaller atomic radius

questions.hint: The 2p electron in boron is higher in energy and more easily removed than Be's 2s electron.
questions.explanation: Be has configuration 1s²2s²; its 2s electrons are tightly held. B has 1s²2s²2p¹; the 2p electron is higher in energy and more shielded, so easier to remove. Option A is false (Z_eff is higher for B).

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 1: ATOMIC STRUCTURE AND PERIODIC PROPERTIES OF THE ELEMENTS
source_trace.section_anchor: 1.8 Electronic Configurations and the Periodic Table of the Elements
source_trace.content_anchor: Boron has a smaller first ionization energy than beryllium

explanations_tiered.definition: Ionization energy generally increases across a period, but drops from Be to B because B's outermost electron is in a p orbital (higher energy, more shielded).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Be: [He]2s² (2s electron removed). B: [He]2s²2p¹ (2p electron removed). 2p orbital is higher in energy and less penetrating, so easier to remove.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Boron's extra electron sits in a 'lazy' p orbital, further from nucleus, so it's easier to knock off than beryllium's tight s electrons.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Shielding and penetration: 2s electrons are closer to nucleus and feel more Z_eff. The 2p electron in B is shielded by 2s² and is higher in energy, lowering IE₁.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Quantum mechanically, the 2p electron has lower probability density near nucleus (less penetration) than 2s, so it is less tightly bound despite higher nuclear charge.
reasoning_steps: Recall Be configuration: [He]2s².
B configuration: [He]2s²2p¹.
2p electron is higher energy and easier to remove than 2s.
misconceptions: Some think IE always increases strictly — but exceptions occur due to subshell stability (Be has full 2s) and p orbital penetration.
Higher Z_eff of B would suggest higher IE, but orbital energy difference dominates.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: B < Be IE because B removes 2p (higher energy).

glossary_entry.definition: IE drops from Be to B because B's outermost electron is in a higher-energy 2p orbital.

flashcard.front: Why is IE of B lower than Be?
flashcard.back: B removes a 2p electron (higher energy, easier).

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Which has lower IE: Be or B? Why?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Be to B drop due to p orbital removal.

concept_map_nodes.relation: drops from Be to B because

publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Boron's 2p electron is higher in energy and more shielded.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.unit: Unit 1: ATOMIC STRUCTURE AND PERIODIC PROPERTIES OF THE ELEMENTS
questions.section: 1.8 Electronic Configurations and the Periodic Table of the Elements
questions.question: Which group of elements has the most negative (most exothermic) electron affinities?
questions.hint: Adding an electron to a halogen completes a stable octet.
questions.explanation: Halogens have the most exothermic (negative) electron affinities because adding an electron gives them a full ns²np⁶ noble gas configuration. Group 1 values are less negative; noble gases have positive or zero EA.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 1: ATOMIC STRUCTURE AND PERIODIC PROPERTIES OF THE ELEMENTS
source_trace.section_anchor: 1.8 Electronic Configurations and the Periodic Table of the Elements
source_trace.content_anchor: Group VIIA elements have the largest negative electron affinities

explanations_tiered.definition: Electron affinity is the energy released when an atom gains an electron. Halogens release the most energy (most negative EA).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Halogens have one electron less than a noble gas configuration, so gaining an electron gives a stable octet, releasing substantial energy.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Chlorine really wants one more electron to become stable like argon — it releases a lot of energy when it gets it.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: EA values (kJ/mol): Cl = -349, F = -328, Br = -325, I = -295. These are the most negative among all groups.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The trend in EA across a period increases (more negative) toward halogens, then drops to zero or positive for noble gases (which resist adding electrons).
reasoning_steps: Recall EA becomes more negative left to right until noble gas.
Halogens (Group 17) are just before noble gases.
They have most negative EA.
misconceptions: Some think noble gases have negative EA — they have positive or near zero.
Fluorine has slightly less negative EA than chlorine due to small size and electron repulsion.

glossary_entry.definition: Most exothermic electron affinities occur in Group 17 (halogens).

flashcard.front: Which group has most negative electron affinity?

concept_map_nodes.relation: release most energy when gaining e⁻ because

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Why do halogens have high electron affinity?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Gaining an electron gives stable octet, releasing energy.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Energy change when atom gains an electron.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Halogens have the most negative electron affinities.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.unit: Unit 1: ATOMIC STRUCTURE AND PERIODIC PROPERTIES OF THE ELEMENTS
questions.section: 1.8 Electronic Configurations and the Periodic Table of the Elements
questions.question: Which element has the highest electronegativity on the Pauling scale?
questions.hint: Electronegativity increases up and to the right, peaking at fluorine.
questions.explanation: Fluorine has the highest electronegativity (4.0). Chlorine is 3.0, oxygen 3.5, nitrogen 3.0. Trend: increases across period, decreases down group.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 1: ATOMIC STRUCTURE AND PERIODIC PROPERTIES OF THE ELEMENTS
source_trace.section_anchor: 1.8 Electronic Configurations and the Periodic Table of the Elements
source_trace.content_anchor: Linus Pauling ... assigned arbitrarily a value of 4.0 to fluorine

explanations_tiered.definition: Electronegativity is a measure of an atom's ability to attract shared electrons in a chemical bond.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Fluorine has the highest electronegativity (4.0) because it is small, has high Z_eff, and needs only one electron to complete its octet.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Fluorine is the 'electron hog' — it pulls shared electrons more strongly than any other element.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Pauling scale: F = 4.0, O = 3.5, Cl = 3.0, N = 3.0. Trend: increases up and right, F is top right corner.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Electronegativity correlates with ionization energy and electron affinity. Fluorine's small radius and high Z_eff give it the greatest electron-attracting power.
reasoning_steps: Recall electronegativity trend: increases across period, up group.
Fluorine is at top right (excluding noble gases).
Fluorine has highest electronegativity.
misconceptions: Some think chlorine has highest — but fluorine is higher despite chlorine's larger size.
Noble gases have undefined electronegativity (not assigned).

glossary_entry.definition: Ability of an atom to attract shared electrons in a bond.

publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Fluorine has highest electronegativity (4.0).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.unit: Unit 1: ATOMIC STRUCTURE AND PERIODIC PROPERTIES OF THE ELEMENTS
questions.section: 1.8 Electronic Configurations and the Periodic Table of the Elements
questions.question: Which of the following elements has the strongest metallic character?
questions.hint: Metallic character increases down a group and decreases across a period to the right.
questions.explanation: Metallic character decreases left to right across Period 3. Sodium (leftmost) is most metallic, silicon is a metalloid. Na > Mg > Al > Si.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 1: ATOMIC STRUCTURE AND PERIODIC PROPERTIES OF THE ELEMENTS
source_trace.section_anchor: 1.8 Electronic Configurations and the Periodic Table of the Elements
source_trace.content_anchor: Metallic character decreases as you move across a period from left to right

explanations_tiered.definition: Metallic character refers to how easily an atom loses electrons to form positive ions.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Metals are on the left side of the periodic table. Across a period, metallic character decreases as elements become more nonmetallic.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Sodium is a shiny, conductive metal; silicon is a dull metalloid. Sodium is much more metallic.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Period 3: Na (metal), Mg (metal), Al (metal), Si (metalloid), P (nonmetal), S (nonmetal), Cl (nonmetal), Ar (noble gas). Na is most metallic.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Metallic character correlates with low ionization energy and large atomic radius. Na has lowest IE₁ in Period 3, so it loses electrons most easily.
reasoning_steps: Recall metallic character decreases left to right.
Sodium is leftmost in Period 3 among options.
Therefore Na is most metallic.
misconceptions: Some think Al is more metallic than Na — false; Al is less metallic.
Metallic character is not the same as conductivity alone; it's about electron loss tendency.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Metallic character ↓ across period. Na most metallic in Period 3.

glossary_entry.definition: Tendency of an element to lose electrons and form positive ions.

flashcard.front: Which Period 3 element is most metallic?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Which is more metallic: Na or Si?
publishing_metadata.revision_note: Na > Mg > Al > Si in metallic character.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Sodium is most metallic among Period 3 options.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.unit: Unit 1: ATOMIC STRUCTURE AND PERIODIC PROPERTIES OF THE ELEMENTS
questions.section: 1.8 Electronic Configurations and the Periodic Table of the Elements
questions.question: Naturally occurring lithium consists of 6.01512 amu and 7.01600 amu isotopes. If the atomic mass of lithium is 6.941 amu, calculate the fractional abundance of Li-7.
questions.hint: Use weighted average: atomic mass = (mass₁ × fraction₁) + (mass₂ × fraction₂).

calculation_steps: Let x = fractional abundance of Li-7, then (1-x) = abundance of Li-6

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 1: ATOMIC STRUCTURE AND PERIODIC PROPERTIES OF THE ELEMENTS
source_trace.section_anchor: 1.8 Electronic Configurations and the Periodic Table of the Elements
source_trace.content_anchor: atomic mass = isotopic mass × fractional abundance

explanations_tiered.definition: Atomic mass is the weighted average of isotopic masses based on their fractional abundances.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: The equation A = f₁A₁ + f₂A₂ with f₁ + f₂ = 1 allows calculation of unknown abundance.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Like calculating your grade: different tests have different weights. Here, Li-7 has a high weight (0.925).
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Set x = fraction of Li-7. Then 6.01512(1-x) + 7.01600x = 6.941. Solving gives x = 0.925.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The small rounding in the equation yields x ≈ 0.925. This shows Li-7 is the dominant isotope (92.5%).
worked_solution: Step 1: Let fraction of Li-7 = x, Li-6 = 1-x.
reasoning_steps: Let x = abundance of Li-7.
Set up weighted average equation.
Solve for x.
misconceptions: Some use arithmetic mean instead of weighted average.
Forgetting that sum of abundances = 1.

glossary_entry.definition: Average mass of isotopes weighted by their natural abundances.

flashcard.front: How to find fractional abundance from atomic mass?
flashcard.back: Set up weighted average equation and solve.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Calculate Li-7 abundance from atomic mass 6.941 amu.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Calculate atomic mass of naturally occurring isotopic elements

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.unit: Unit 1: ATOMIC STRUCTURE AND PERIODIC PROPERTIES OF THE ELEMENTS
questions.section: 1.8 Electronic Configurations and the Periodic Table of the Elements
questions.question: Why does atomic radius increase from top to bottom within a group?

options.A: Electrons are added to higher principal energy levels (n)

questions.hint: Each row down adds a new electron shell.
questions.explanation: Down a group, each element has an additional principal energy level (higher n). The valence electrons are farther from the nucleus, increasing atomic radius. Nuclear charge increases, but shell addition dominates.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 1: ATOMIC STRUCTURE AND PERIODIC PROPERTIES OF THE ELEMENTS
source_trace.section_anchor: 1.8 Electronic Configurations and the Periodic Table of the Elements
source_trace.content_anchor: Atomic radius generally increases in a group from top to bottom

explanations_tiered.definition: Atomic radius increases down a group because each successive element has an additional electron shell (higher n).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Even though nuclear charge increases, the effect of adding a new shell (increased distance) outweighs the increased pull.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Like stacking boxes: each new row (shell) makes the stack taller, even if the boxes are slightly heavier.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Li (n=2) radius 152 pm, Na (n=3) 186 pm, K (n=4) 227 pm, Rb (n=5) 248 pm, Cs (n=6) 265 pm. Each step adds a shell.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Shielding increases significantly down a group because inner electrons screen the valence electrons from the full nuclear charge, so Z_eff increases only slightly despite larger Z.
reasoning_steps: Recall radius increases down group.
Each element down has electrons in higher n shell.
Higher n means farther average distance from nucleus.
misconceptions: Some think nuclear charge increase alone would shrink atoms — but shell addition dominates.
Shielding is more effective down a group.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Radius ↑ down group: new shell added.

glossary_entry.definition: Atomic radius increases down a group due to addition of principal energy levels.

flashcard.front: Why does atomic radius increase down a group?
flashcard.back: Electrons fill higher principal energy levels (n).

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Why is Cs larger than Li?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Cs has more electron shells (higher n).
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Radius increases due to additional shells.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Explain general trends in atomic radius within a group
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Higher principal quantum number increases distance.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.unit: Unit 1: ATOMIC STRUCTURE AND PERIODIC PROPERTIES OF THE ELEMENTS
questions.section: 1.8 Electronic Configurations and the Periodic Table of the Elements
questions.question: The modern periodic law states that properties of elements are a periodic function of their ______.
questions.hint: Henry Moseley established the relationship with atomic number.
questions.explanation: Modern periodic law (Moseley, 1913): properties repeat periodically when elements are arranged by increasing atomic number, not atomic mass (Mendeleev's original).

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 1: ATOMIC STRUCTURE AND PERIODIC PROPERTIES OF THE ELEMENTS
source_trace.section_anchor: 1.8 Electronic Configurations and the Periodic Table of the Elements
source_trace.content_anchor: periodic law states that certain sets of physical and chemical properties repeat ... when arranged according to increasing atomic number

explanations_tiered.definition: The modern periodic law: properties of elements are periodic functions of their atomic number.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Mendeleev used atomic mass, but Moseley showed atomic number (proton count) is the fundamental basis for periodicity.
explanations_tiered.beginner: It's the atomic number (protons) that determines where an element sits and how it behaves, not its weight.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Arranging by atomic number corrects anomalies like Ar (18, 40 amu) before K (19, 39 amu).
explanations_tiered.advanced: Moseley's X-ray spectroscopy showed linear relationship between √(frequency) and atomic number, establishing Z as the basis of the periodic table.
reasoning_steps: Recall modern periodic law vs Mendeleev's.
Atomic number (Z) is the modern basis.
Select atomic number.
misconceptions: Some still think atomic mass determines periodic properties — modern law uses atomic number.
Mass number is not the same as atomic mass.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Modern periodic law: properties function of atomic number (Z).

glossary_entry.definition: Physical and chemical properties of elements repeat periodically when arranged by increasing atomic number.

flashcard.front: What is the basis of modern periodic law?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What is the modern periodic law based on?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Properties repeat with increasing atomic number.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: Periodic law: function of atomic number.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Contrast with Mendeleev's atomic mass basis.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Modern periodic law is based on atomic number.
